utterances: OK.
So North Korea - nuclear weapons, wouldn't that be a shared item of interest and a very pressing issue on the agenda for both countries?
Is there anything that they can really hope to accomplish in this meeting?
Well, they've got to do something about it because time is really running out before North Korea will get missiles with nuclear warheads capable of targeting U.S.
territory.
The problem is there is so much daylight between the U.S.'s and China's positions.
Both sides think the other side has to do the heavy lifting on this problem.
As far as Beijing is concerned, it wants the U.S.
to provide North Korea with some sort of security guarantee in exchange for either freezing its nuclear programs or denuclearizing, as it's already promised to do.
The other problem is that both sides realize that the North Korean nuclear issue is tied into so many other things.
So if Washington and Beijing get into a spat over trade, over Taiwan, over the South China Sea, the chances of cooperation on the North Korean nuclear issue will be very slim.
That was NPR's Beijing correspondent Anthony Kuhn joining us from Beijing.
Anthony, thanks so much for speaking with us.
You bet, Michel.
On public college campuses in Texas, students over 21 who are licensed gun holders can now bring their weapons into dorms and classrooms as long as their firearms are hidden away, say, in a backpack.
It's a controversial law that took effect last week, and three professors at the University of Texas at Austin are out to stop it.
They'll be in court this week asking a judge to hold off on the law while they try to get it overturned in the courts.
The clock is ticking, as classes start there on August 24.
Lisa Moore is one of the professors.
She teaches English and gender studies at the University of Texas at Austin, and she joins us from her office.
Professor, welcome.
Thank you so much.
Why did you and your colleagues decide to bring this lawsuit?
We just find that it's impossible to do our jobs with this policy in place.

We all teach subject matter that is quite sensitive, and we all use very participatory, you know, pedagogically sound methods of trying to teach students how to state their views on controversial subjects, challenge one another and stand up for what they believe in.
And the thing is our classrooms have to be laboratories for experimenting with those kinds of challenging materials and challenging stances that people take.
So you're saying that these things can't be done if someone has a firearm in the room?
I'm saying that - yeah, that's what I'm saying because it's my job to create a safe learning environment for students in order to encourage them to take those kinds of intellectual risks.
And I am genuinely not equipped to keep students safe from a firearm in my classroom.
One of the provisions of this law is that in order to minimize the amount of handling of a gun that goes on on campus, the gun has to be already loaded before you bring it into the classroom.
So it is cocked.
It - there's a chambered round in a gun that might be in someone's backpack.

And already this week - I've been teaching summer school, and my students are talking about their concern that someone sitting next to them is going to drop their bag and there'll be an accidental misfire that will cause an accident.
These are 18 to 22-year-olds, OK?
Say the licensed gun holders are 21 and over.
These are people who are at a very vulnerable stage of life where there's a lot of struggles with the onset of adult mental illness.
There's often a lot of poor choices made with regards to drugs and alcohol.
I've had students show up to my classes in all kinds of states where they might not be making the best choices.
These are not circumstances in which we want guns in the mix.
By making this move, you know you've picked a fight with some of the most powerful people in Texas.
The law is supported by the governor, Greg Abbott.
The attorney general says the law is absolutely constitutionally sound and he'll defend it in court.
What's ahead?
Well, you know, they may be politically powerful, but there has always been a powerful strain of Texas populism as well.
And I consider myself to be in that tradition.
I also know that there's power in the intellectual power of everything that goes on on a University of Texas campus and all the other public university campuses in the state.
Everyone from the chancellor, Admiral McRaven, the guy who got bin Laden, you know, on down the line in the University of Texas system is against this law.
We, too, have power.
Senator Feingold even wanted to know if the president could assassinate an American citizen in the interests of national security.
Such overly general questions don't really help matters.

My answer, of course, was simply no. I left it to the packed hearing room to figure out how the answer might change if that citizen was an al-Qaida confederate about to explode a dirty bomb on the subway.
The president and Congress alike should not use exaggerated hypotheticals to press the limits of their powers.
Doing so can have bad consequences.
The president discovered this with his since discarded torture memo, which some claim contributed to prisoner abuse.
It's equally important for Congress not to dwell on exotic issues like assassination, lest real opportunities to limit al-Qaida be lost.
There's a tentative deal allowing Senate oversight of the terrorist surveillance program.
It will work only if both branches avoid claims of inflated authority.
In the tug of war on terror over the scope of presidential power, Congress is especially fond of quoting Supreme Court Justice Jackson's opinion in the 1952 Youngstown case.
He told Harry Truman he could not settle a labor dispute by seizing steel mills, even if it would help with the Korean conflict.
Justice Jackson explained his reasoning by putting presidential power into three boxes.
When the president acts in agreement with Congress, he is at the zenith of his authority.
When Congress has said nothing about an issue, the president was said to be, with apologies to Rod Serling, in a zone of twilight.
And when the president acts contrary to Congress on his own authority, he is at his lowest ebb.
Today's Congress should remember what Justice Felix Frankfurter said as well.
He concurred in Youngstown, but disagreed with Jackson's broader theorizing.
He wrote that war power issues should be met without attempting to define the president's power comprehensively.

It's unprofitable, he said, to conjure up hypothetical future cases, and it ought to be a common understanding that clashes between the different branches of government should be avoided if a legal ground of less-explosive potentialities is properly available.
As Frankfurter suspected, Jackson's three boxes don't resolve tough cases.
President Bush, in his role as Commander-In-Chief, and with Congress's post-9/11 force resolution, ranks himself in the topmost powerful box.
Bush's opponents, by contrast, consign him to the bottom drawer.
Frankfurter was right.
Such intramural sparring over the outer reaches of power only serves to divide.
In brokering a deal, both Congress and the president have recognized the need for enhanced enemy surveillance.
If there ever was a time when external threat requires mutual resolve, it is now.
Commentary from Douglas Kmiec, professor of constitutional law at Pepperdine University.
This is MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Renee Montagne, with Steve Inskeep.
The recent terrorist bombings in London and Sharm el-Sheikh, Egypt, have again focused the world's attention on violent Islamist extremists.
To see how media outlets in the Arab world are covering these attacks, Steve Inskeep called a journalism professor; we check in with him from time to time.
Ramez Maluf has been monitoring the Arab media from his post at the Lebanese American University in Beirut, and he joins us once again to tell us what he's seeing and reading and hearing.
Welcome to the program.
Well, my pleasure.
Of course, there have been quite a few bombings and attempted bombings in recent weeks.
Let's talk first about the attacks on London.
What has the tone been, generally, of coverage of those bombings?
I believe that there has been a real turning point in the tone.
Increasingly there is concern with the image of Islam in the West, much more so than before.
There's less attention to trying to defend the bombings as something brought about by the bad policies of the West.
Is this a normal swing of the pendulum?

When there is an attack against the West, people's attitudes change, as you say, and then when that attack becomes more distant and perhaps something like American abuses is more in the headlines, the views shift back again?
Well, I think it's probably more concern with this idea of equating Islam with terrorism.
I mean, let me give you an example.
Earlier this week, there was a very interesting program on Al-Jazeera.
The subject of this talk show was: Is Islam causing these terror attacks or are there political issues that are making Muslims behave in this way?
And this question was posed to the public, so people had a chance to vote.

And at the end of the program, they gave the result of the polls, and the polls showed that 36 percent of the people that called in to vote said that the reason for the terror attacks was the teachings of Islam.

Now I find these figures amazing, that the viewers of this program were willing to say that there was something in the way Islam was being taught, or something as well as these participants in the debate were saying that was intrinsic to Islam itself.

We should mention, I suppose, these weren't scientific numbers, but nevertheless, you're pointing out some number of viewers of this program were willing to reconsider what's happening inside their own religion.
Right, right.
But these are normal viewers of Al-Jazeera, I assume, who normally would be very virulent, you know, anti-Western.
And the argument that was being made during this program was that the Islamic terrorists were actually resorting to citing scripture, Islamic scripture, to justify their attacks.
So they were equating terror with Islam.
It was their doing.
Do you see other signs of this attitude as you look through Arabic newspapers or flip through Arabic channels?
Do you know - does anyone know how this law is affecting recruitment, retention, both the faculty and students?
There's no better example of this than the law that has become one of the emptiest slogans in the history of American politics - No Child Left Behind.
Scoring for No Child Left Behind appears to be one piece of this issue that does work - for Democrats, anyway.
Teachers' unions remain outraged over the impact of President Bush's signature education law and are determined to see it abolished or radically restructured.
Jack Jennings, with the Center on Education Policy, says NCLB gets Democrats salivating for a simple reason.
Bush is so identified with No Child Left Behind that when he has 28 percent approval, it means that anything he's identified with is going to go down in regard.
And I think that teachers and others are piling on No Child Left Behind partially because they don't like Bush.
(Soundbite of CNN/YouTube debate)
Governor Richardson, you have had to implement No Child Left Behind in your state.
Would you scrap it?
Revise it?
I would scrap it.
It doesn't work.
(Soundbite of applause)
What Governor Richardson did not say in the YouTube debate is that some of the biggest supporters of the law are Democrats in Congress.
Democrats helped pass the law in President Bush's first term and Dems now chair the Education Committee.
Most education watchers put the chances for repealing No Child Left Behind somewhere between slim and none.
Republicans have tried even more gently on the education issue.
Mitt Romney summed up why that is in New Hampshire earlier this year.
I generally like schools to be managed by local communities and states, not the federal government.
And I don't want the federal government telling people what to teach in schools and how to run schools.
So I'm - I've got a president that gets the federal government too involved in education.
Now, of course, the candidates' uneasy relationship with education may change once the primaries are over.
But if past history is any guide, the presidential candidates will continue to treat this top-tier issue as an extension of the age-old campaign tradition - kissing babies.
Larry Abramson, NPR News, Washington.
How far can President Trump go in defying the will of Congress?
